sanskrit: अथ व्यवस्थितान् दृष्ट्वा धार्तराष्ट्रान् कपिध्वजः। प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसम्पाते धनुरुद्यम्य पाण्डवः। हृषीकेशं तदा वाक्यमिदमाह महीपते।
transliteration: atha vyavasthitān dṛṣṭvā dhārtarāṣṭrān kapi-dhvajaḥ pravṛtte śastra-sampāte dhanur udyamya pāṇḍavaḥ hṛṣīkeśaṃ tadā vākyam idam āha mahī-pate
literal_meaning: Then, seeing the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra arrayed, the monkey-bannered Pāṇḍava lifted his bow as the weapons were about to clash, and spoke these words to Hṛṣīkeśa.
contextual_meaning: Arjuna stands on the razor-edge between peace and violence, his body poised for war while his mind begins to fracture under the weight of what he must do. The act of raising his bow is not merely military readiness—it is the moment the psyche splits between duty and conscience, where the conscious self still believes it can proceed while the unconscious already foresees the moral injury about to unfold.

shadow: Grief for the Self That Must Die

psychological_state: The dissociative calm before the psyche shatters—when one is still functioning but can feel the approaching collapse of meaning.
trigger_scenario: A surgeon scrubbing in for the first high-risk operation after a patient death, hands steady while the mind whispers 'what if I kill again?'
anchor_text: Pause before the first irreversible act; the soul speaks when the bow is raised.
